title: Burmese in Thailand

([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ชาวพม่าในไทย, [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"): ထိုင်းနိုင်ငံရှိမြန်မာများ) constitute Thailand's largest migrant population. According to the [2014 Myanmar Census](/wiki/2014_Myanmar_Census "2014 Myanmar Census"), 1,418,472 former Burmese residents, including 812,798 men and 605,674 women, were living in Thailand, constituting about 70% of Burma's overseas population.[[1]](#cite_note-cens-1) Burmese in Thailand tend to fall into three categories: professional migrants working in the business or professional sectors, laborers working in low-skilled professions, and refugees fleeing conflict.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Ethnic group

Burmese in Thailand

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar")[Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") | |
| Ethnic [Shans](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people") celebrating the Poi Sang Long Festival in [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son "Mae Hong Son") | |
| Total population | |
| 1,418,472 (2014)[[1]](#cite_note-cens-1) | |
| Regions with significant populations | |
| * [Samut Sakhon](/wiki/Samut_Sakhon_province "Samut Sakhon province") * [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_province "Mae Hong Son province") * [Tak](/wiki/Tak_province "Tak province") * [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok") * [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_province "Chiang Mai province") * [Ranong](/wiki/Ranong_province "Ranong province") | |
| Languages | |
| [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"), [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language"), [Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"), [Northern Thai](/wiki/Northern_Thai_language "Northern Thai language") and various languages of Myanmar (native), [Central Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") ([lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")) | |
| Religion | |
| * [Theravada Buddhism](/wiki/Theravada_Buddhism "Theravada Buddhism") * Christianity * Islam | |
| Related ethnic groups | |
| [Burmese diaspora](/wiki/Burmese_diaspora "Burmese diaspora"), [Bamar](/wiki/Bamar_people "Bamar people"), [Karen](/wiki/Karen_people "Karen people"), [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people"), [Shan](/wiki/Shan_people "Shan people") | |

**Burmese in Thailand** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ชาวพม่าในไทย, [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"): ထိုင်းနိုင်ငံရှိမြန်မာများ) constitute Thailand's largest migrant population. According to the [2014 Myanmar Census](/wiki/2014_Myanmar_Census "2014 Myanmar Census"), 1,418,472 former Burmese residents, including 812,798 men and 605,674 women, were living in Thailand, constituting about 70% of Burma's overseas population.[[1]](#cite_note-cens-1) Burmese in Thailand tend to fall into three categories: professional migrants working in the business or professional sectors, laborers working in low-skilled professions, and refugees fleeing conflict.

[Migrant workers](/wiki/Migrant_worker "Migrant worker") tend to hold low-skilled jobs in the fishing and seafood processing, construction, garment, and domestic service industries.[[2]](#cite_note-mcgann-2) [Macquarie University](/wiki/Macquarie_University "Macquarie University") estimates that the average annual remittances from Thailand to Burma exceed US$300 million.[[3]](#cite_note-irin-3)[[4]](#cite_note-turnell-4) The movement of Burmese nationals into Thailand began in the 1970s, following the [1962 Burmese coup d'état](/wiki/1962_Burmese_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1962 Burmese coup d'état") and resulting economic decline from implementation of the [Burmese Way to Socialism](/wiki/Burmese_Way_to_Socialism "Burmese Way to Socialism"), and ongoing civil conflicts.[[5]](#cite_note-vicary-5) Burmese migrants contribute tremendously to the [Thai economy](/wiki/Thai_economy "Thai economy"), contributing between 5 and 6.2% of Thailand's [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP").[[6]](#cite_note-brees-6)

[Samut Sakhon province](/wiki/Samut_Sakhon_province "Samut Sakhon province") is home to Thailand's largest Burmese migrant community, representing about 200,000 Burmese migrants.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Other large Burmese communities reside in [Mae Sot](/wiki/Mae_Sot "Mae Sot").[[9]](#cite_note-ms-9) [Ranong](/wiki/Ranong "Ranong"),[[10]](#cite_note-moncrief-10)

In 2003, the Thai and Burmese governments signed a memorandum of understanding to formally recognize this labor migration flow and legalize migration through a government program to recruit workers directly from Burma, and to use a nationality verification process whereby migrant workers receive a temporary passport, an identity certificate, a visa to remain in Thailand for two years, and a change of work status to legal.[[2]](#cite_note-mcgann-2)

There are also roughly 150,000 Burmese refugees living at 9 official camps on the Thai–Burmese border.[[11]](#cite_note-ref-11) The largest such camp is [Mae La refugee camp](/wiki/Mae_La_refugee_camp "Mae La refugee camp"). In 2014, the Thai government announced plans to repatriate Burmese refugees who have been living in border camps for the past two decades.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
